findings.description: Email/SMTP header injection (CWE-93): _render hand-builds raw RFC822 headers via f-string interpolation of unvalidated msg.subject/msg.to/msg.body joined by CRLF, so a subject or to value containing \r\n injects arbitrary headers (Bcc, Reply-To) and body, enabling spam relay, recipient exfiltration, and message spoofing.

Unvalidated msg.to is passed both as the SMTP envelope recipient and interpolated into rendered DATA; CRLF/header-injection payloads in 'to' can smuggle extra recipients/headers into the transmitted message (CWE-93), and 'to' is never validated as a single well-formed address.

MIMEText is imported but unused; the safe email-construction API that would encode headers and prevent injection is bypassed in favor of manual string concatenation in _render, which is the root cause of the header-injection exposure.

Cleartext SMTP submission (CWE-319): flush opens plain smtplib.SMTP on port 587 with no starttls() and no authentication, exposing message bodies and recipients to network eavesdropping and submitting unauthenticated (mitigated only if smtp.internal is a trusted relay on a private segment).

Dedup keys solely on msg.to, so a second distinct message to an already-seen recipient is silently dropped (counted as skipped); a security-adjacent availability/correctness quirk rather than an exploit — defer detail to the logic critic.
